figures.caption: Figure 4: Routing example through the dual-system kernel. System 1 nominal routes (green arrows) handle SUCC transitions, while System 2 learned recovery transitions (red arrows) handle FAIL, BLOCK, and MISS by escalating to alternative specialists or rerouting to fill missing dependencies.

Table 23: Composite Case 1: Insurance claim verification (Event Analysis, SPATIAL+TEMPORAL). Query: Is the vehicle trajectory within the parking zone? Does the claimed time window [1.5, 4.5] fall during the active window [0.0, 6.0]? GT: spatial=1, temporal=1.

Table 24: Composite Case 2: Weather monitoring (Event Analysis, SPATIAL+TEMPORAL). Query: Does the storm trajectory intersect the sensor area? Does the storm’s time [1.0, 3.0] overlap with maintenance [1.0, 2.5]? GT: spatial=1, temporal=1.
Table 25: Case A: execution trace. Route: HEAD SUCC−−−→ SP SUCC−−−→ FUSE; predicted 1 = GT.

Figure 5: Empirical precision–coverage trade-off of α across three benchmarks (Qwen3-8B, balanced slice). Sweeping α ∈ [0, 1] confirms Proposition 6: recovery coverage is non-decreasing in α while overall accuracy degrades only gently over the useful range. The operating point α = 0.3 used throughout the main text sits in the plateau region where recovery transitions are populated without materially perturbing nominal-path routing. The α = 0 configuration (success-only training) corresponds to the empty-support regime of Theorem 1.

Figure 1: STAR architecture. Queries are parsed into a task profile, the failure-aware routing matrix selects specialists conditioned on the current agent, task type, execution status, and specialists execute through an extract-compute-deposit protocol over a shared blackboard before final fusion.
Table 3: Framework-level ablations. Each row removes a non-routing component from the full system.

Table 2: Per-benchmark EM (%) across eight backbones on EM-eligible queries. For each backbone we report LLM-only (red columns) and STAR (blue columns) per benchmark; cell shading is proportional to the value, so a darker blue cell next to a lighter red cell indicates a larger STAR lift. Five numeric prediction types (two STARK, two STB24, one STB26) are excluded from EM and reported in Table 15; STAR “±” values are 95% Wilson half-widths (App. I).

Table 28: Case D: recovery via MISS. Route: HEAD SUCC−−−→ SP MISS−−→ FUSESys. 2; predicted [1.0] = GT. The primary SP call cannot resolve named landmarks without a gazetteer of SF points of interest, so it emits MISS rather than fabricating coordinates. The System 2 row of the transition matrix routes (SP, MISS, STARK_LANDMARK_DIRECTION) 7→ FUSE because the query text alone is sufficient for a world-knowledge answer.

Table 22: Agent computation menus Ma. Each agent exposes a declarative list of deterministic functions; the LLM selects which to invoke and extracts parameters. Implementation details: spatial computations use Shapely and NumPy; temporal computations implement Allen’s Interval Algebra; graph computations use NetworkX with custom causality modules; navigation uses Dijkstra’s algorithm.

Table 14: Per-task results on STARK (Qwen3-8B, N = 2,912; EM=73.0% on Nem = 2,792 EMeligible queries, excluding SPATIOTEMPORAL_FORECAST and TEMPORAL_TRACKING). EM is reported with 95% Wilson CIs. ⋆: numeric prediction task; EM is not meaningful (free-form real-valued output almost never matches the gold string under exact-match normalization) and is therefore omitted; accuracy is reported via RMSE/RMSPE in Table 15 and in the main text. Lat: mean latency. Tok: mean total tokens. LLM/q: mean LLM calls per query.
Table 12: Per-benchmark EM (%) across eight backbone models under STAR (EM-eligible queries, N = 6,363).

Figure 6: Learned transition matrix M averaged across task types, rendered as four status-conditional heatmaps (SUCCESS / FAIL / INFO_MISSING / BLOCKED). Rows index the from_agent, columns index the to_agent, and each cell is P (next | from, status). Each benchmark produces a qualitatively different SUCCESS map (because task taxonomies differ), but every benchmark exhibits the same structural property—error-state rows have non-zero support concentrated on different successors from the SUCCESS row, which is only possible because unsuccessful traces are retained during training (Theorem 1). BLOCKED transitions are populated by the hand-curated safety-net table in System 1 plus any task-specific BLOCKED rules present in the matrix.
Table 27: Case C: external-API tool trace. Route: HEAD SUCC−−−→ SP SUCC−−−→ FUSE; predicted 4 = GT.
Table 4: Router ablations (Qwen3-8B). Overall EM reports accuracy on all queries, in Difference positive = STAR better.

Table 5: Worked example of autonomous failure recovery on a STARK LANDMARK_DIRECTION query (id stark_direction_questions_37; full trace in App. M.4). The primary specialist cannot resolve named landmarks without a gazetteer and emits MISS; the router consults M[LM_DIR, SP, MISS, ·] and dispatches FUSE directly. No human intervention or pre-coded fallback is involved—the recovery transition is entirely learned from training traces.

Figure 2: State-conditioned routing matrix slices for a representative task type. Each panel visualizes a row M[a, s, t, ·] with fixed current agent a and task type t but different execution status s. The differing successor distributions show that recovery is conditioned on the type of failure rather than treated as a generic retry action.

Table 16: Per-category results on STB24 (Qwen3-8B, N = 2,995; EM=70.9% on Nem = 2,443 EM-eligible queries, excluding FLOW_PREDICTION and TRAJECTORY_PREDICTION; 95% Wilson CIs). ⋆: numeric prediction task; EM omitted and regression metric reported separately in Table 15. TRAJECTORY_PREDICTION’s native numeric-tolerance EM would score ∼100% because of a coarse lat/lon tolerance, but the ground-distance AbsErr is 112.6 m, so EM is not a meaningful accuracy measure for this task.
Table 10: Complete task type taxonomy (35 types across three benchmarks).

Table 26: Case B: multi-agent execution trace. Route: HEAD SUCC−−−→ SP SUCC−−−→ TP SUCC−−−→ FUSE; predicted [0.0] = GT. The SP agent produces an intermediate event interval that the TP agent consumes from the blackboard — without this composition the question is unanswerable by either agent alone.

Figure 7: Top-3 successors per failure status, grouped by originating agent. For every from_agent in each benchmark, the three bars per successor show P (next | from, status) for status ∈ {FAIL, MISS, BLOCK}. The claim visualized here is that the same (from_agent, task_type) routes to different recovery agents depending on the observed error type—the height pattern shifts across the three colored bars within each group. This is the direct empirical counterpart of the statusconditioning ablation in Table 4 (row “No status conditioning”), which collapsed these three bars into one and paid up to 33pp on diverging STARK queries.
Table 6: The three failure-recovery channels learned by M with (Qwen3-8B). Each error state is a first-class routing pathway with its own learned successor distribution.

Table 18: Cross-benchmark generalization: per-type comparison. “Specific”: benchmark-specific matrix. “Cross”: STARK-trained matrix applied to all benchmarks. “Classified As”: how the HEAD agent maps the original type to a STARK type.

Table 19: Cross-model EM (%) on STARK per task type. Bold: best per column. ⋆: numeric prediction column (SPATIOTEMPORAL_FORECAST, TEMPORAL_TRACKING); EM is not meaningful for these free-form real-valued outputs and is shown as “–”. Accuracy on those columns is reported in Table 15. Cell-level 95% Wilson CIs are omitted in this dense layout; the per-benchmark totals in Table 2 carry their own CIs.

Table 20: Cross-model EM (%) on STB24 per task type. Bold: best per column. ⋆: numeric prediction column (TRAJ_PRD, FLOW); EM not meaningful, reported in Table 15. Per-benchmark totals carry 95% Wilson CIs in Table 2.

Figure 3: Failure-aware routing and execution feedback in STAR. Specialists return typed signals such as SUCCESS, BLOCK, MISS, and FAIL; the execution-state blackboard stores these signals and supports subsequent routing and final fusion.
Table 8: STB24: STAR vs. published baselines. ↑/↓ denotes higher/lower better.

Table 9: Recovery analysis by first-agent status on the Qwen3-8B balanced slice. “No failure” denotes queries that never enter an error state. “Recovered EM” is the final-answer accuracy after the corresponding error state is observed and the recovery policy is applied.
